one_line_takeaway: AMIS shows that meta-optimizing the LLM judge's scoring rubric (not just the attack prompt) yields much stronger, more transferable jailbreaks, reaching up to 100% ASR on Claude-Sonnet-4 and 88% on Claude-3.5-Haiku.
key_finding: AMIS sets state-of-the-art jailbreak performance across all five targets on both benchmarks, with the largest gains on the strongly safety-aligned Claude models: 88.0% ASR on Claude-3.5-Haiku and 100.0% ASR on Claude-Sonnet-4 on AdvBench. Ablations show the outer-loop scoring-template evolution and prompt inheritance are both critical, and template optimization is shown to be a more impactful lever than prompt optimization alone. A counterintuitive result: prompts optimized on more strongly safety-aligned models transfer better, and Claude-3.5-Haiku appears more robust to transferred jailbreaks than the newer Claude-Sonnet-4.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AMIS (Align to MISalign), an automatic LLM-jailbreak framework whose novel element is meta-optimizing the LLM judge's scoring template rather than only the attack prompts. AMIS is a bi-level optimization: the inner loop (query-level) refines jailbreak prompts using dense continuous feedback (1.0-10.0 scores) from a fixed scoring template; the outer loop (dataset-level) evolves the scoring template itself so its continuous scores better predict true binary attack success, measured by a newly proposed 'ASR alignment score'. This is the paper's own contribution, distinct from cited prior attackers (PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN/AutoDAN-Turbo, GCG, SeqAR) which optimize prompts against fixed binary ASR signals or hand-crafted scoring rubrics.
threat_model: Red-teaming / adversarial jailbreak setting. An attacker LLM iteratively crafts input prompts to bypass a target LLM's safety alignment and elicit harmful/disallowed content; a judge LLM evaluates target responses. Evaluated in both black-box and white-box access settings against five target models. The stated intent is defensive vulnerability discovery, but the method is an offensive attack that raises ASR against production models (including Claude-3.5-Haiku and Claude-Sonnet-4).
limitations: Author-stated: (1) ASR is measured via an LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o-mini), which can carry judge bias and limit robustness of the reported numbers; (2) the nested inner-outer optimization has non-trivial compute cost (though shown cheaper than PAIR for equal ASR). Reviewer-noted: the same model family (GPT-4o-mini) serves as inner-loop scorer, ASR judge, and template optimizer, so the optimization can partly game its own judge; benchmarks are small (50 AdvBench queries, 100 JBB queries); no defense/mitigation is proposed, only attack. Future work suggested: multi-judge or human-in-the-loop evaluation and more sample-efficient optimization.

quantitative_results: AdvBench ASR (AMIS): Llama-3.1-8B 100.0, GPT-4o-mini 98.0, GPT-4o 100.0, Claude-3.5-Haiku 88.0, Claude-Sonnet-4 100.0
AdvBench StR (AMIS): Llama-3.1-8B 0.84, GPT-4o-mini 0.87, GPT-4o 0.87, Haiku-3.5 0.42, Sonnet-4 0.70
JBB-Behaviors ASR (AMIS): Llama-3.1-8B 100.0, GPT-4o-mini 100.0, GPT-4o 97.0, Haiku-3.5 78.0, Sonnet-4 88.0
JBB-Behaviors StR (AMIS): Llama-3.1-8B 0.95, GPT-4o-mini 0.85, GPT-4o 0.85, Haiku-3.5 0.48, Sonnet-4 0.67
Avg improvement over 2nd-best: AdvBench +26.0% ASR and +0.44 StR; JBB +20.2% ASR and +0.41 StR; abstract cites >70.5 pts avg over baselines on Claude targets

Ablation on AdvBench/Haiku-3.5 (ASR/StR): w/o inner+outer 4.0/0.04, w/o outer 86.0/0.28, w/o dense scoring template 74.0/0.40, w/o dataset-level 84.0/0.35, w/o prompt inheritance 80.0/0.28, full AMIS 88.0/0.42
Scoring-template variants (ASR/StR): ASR binary 0/1 80.0/0.41, StR [0,5] 68.0/0.17, AuT [1,10] initial 86.0/0.28
Cross-dataset transfer (ASR/StR): JBB->AdvBench 86.0/0.42 (vs AdvBench orig 88.0/0.42); AdvBench->JBB 87.0/0.50 (vs JBB orig 78.0/0.48)
Cost: AMIS reaches 100% ASR at ~$1.96/query on Claude-Sonnet-4/AdvBench, while PAIR reaches only 94% ASR at higher cost
